claimed_contribution: The paper introduces self-complexity as a determinant of preferences for dual- versus single-personality cobrands, beyond traditional parent-brand fit accounts.
It provides evidence that activating the more complex self-aspect (independence for Caucasians, interdependence for Hispanics) increases favorability toward a sophisticated-and-exciting cobrand.
It offers initial evidence that the self-complexity effect on cobrand evaluation is conscious rather than automatic, using a cognitive-load test.
context: The study is set in cobranding, where two parent brands are combined and the resulting cobrand can reflect one dominant personality dimension or a blend of two. Experiments use student consumers evaluating cobrands in ad-based scenarios (mainly fashion clothing; later a cruise-hotel vacation package) and compare Caucasian and Hispanic samples.
design_and_data: The paper reports three between-subjects experiments using ad stimuli that manipulate cobrand personality structure (single: sophistication-sophistication or excitement-excitement; dual: sophistication-excitement) and self activation. Experiment 1 (N = 106 undergraduates) manipulates self-referencing (high vs low) and measures ad/cobrand evaluations plus coded thought listings. Experiment 2 (N = 85 Caucasian undergraduates) primes independent vs interdependent self before ad exposure and codes self-related and complexity-related thought content, including mediation tests. Experiment 3 (N = 61 Hispanic undergraduates) primes self-construal and manipulates cognitive load (1 vs 25 words to memorize) while evaluating a dual-personality vacation-package cobrand; a follow-up with 66 Caucasian students replicates the cognitive-load pattern.
main_findings: When self-referencing was high, participants evaluated dual-personality cobrands more favorably than single-personality cobrands, but this difference did not appear under low self-referencing.

Among Caucasian participants, priming independence (the relatively more complex self-aspect) increased evaluations of dual-personality cobrands versus single-personality alternatives, whereas priming interdependence did not.

In the Hispanic sample, interdependence priming increased dual-cobrand evaluations only under low cognitive load, and this prime effect was reduced under high load; thought-complexity measures and mediation analyses supported the proposed self-complexity process.
one_line_summary: Across three experiments, the paper shows that activating a consumer's more complex self-aspect increases preference for a dual-personality cobrand (sophisticated plus exciting), and that this effect depends on available cognitive resources.
relation_to_prior_work: extends cobranding and brand personality research by moving beyond parent-brand fit and introducing self-complexity as a mechanism shaping evaluations of blended cobrand personalities.
research_gap: Prior cobranding work focused mainly on blending functional/product attributes and parent-brand fit, with limited attention to symbolic-emotional brand personality effects. The literature had not examined how the self, especially self-complexity, influences evaluations of dual- versus single-personality cobrands.
research_question: How does self-complexity influence consumer evaluations of cobrands that blend two distinct brand personality dimensions versus one dimension? Specifically, do consumers prefer dual-personality cobrands when a more complex aspect of the self is activated, and is this process conscious or nonconscious?
